summary: Brentwood was the site of the first major labor organizing efforts among fruit pickers in California back in July 1934. It was there that tensions boiled over into riots and arrests, particularly around the apricot farms. This unrest was part of the early attempts to improve wages and conditions for agricultural workers, and it deeply influenced local politics and law enforcement at the time.

I was elected sheriff of Contra Costa County in 1934, succeeding a man who had held the office for forty years. The county was the backdrop for significant labor unrest during that period, especially involving agricultural workers in places like Brentwood. My role as sheriff placed me right in the middle of these conflicts, and I had to navigate the challenges of transitioning from old-style law enforcement to dealing with new social and labor movements.

entity: Cannery and Agricultural Workers Industrial Union
summary: The Cannery and Agricultural Workers Industrial Union was the name generally associated with the early labor organizing efforts among fruit pickers in California around 1934. While it was still in an embryonic and disorganized stage, possibly originating from Stockton or San Francisco, it represented the first attempts to unionize workers who had never been organized before. Their efforts, though chaotic, sparked significant unrest and brought attention to the poor wages and conditions faced by agricultural laborers.

Stockton may have been one of the places where the Cannery and Agricultural Workers Industrial Union first took shape, although I can't say for certain. It was part of the broader Northern California region where early labor organizing among agricultural workers was beginning to emerge in the early 1930s. The city was connected to the union's embryonic stages alongside San Francisco.
cues: Cannery and Agricultural Workers Industrial Union

summary: San Francisco was another key location linked to the early formation of the Cannery and Agricultural Workers Industrial Union. Along with Stockton, it was part of the region where these labor organizing efforts were just getting started in the early 1930s. Although the union was disorganized and inexperienced, San Francisco played a role in the union's beginnings.

summary: I worked as a fruit picker in the Livermore Valley area from 1898 until 1915, picking grapes, prunes, and almonds. Those were tough years with very poor wages—about a dollar a day for a twelve-hour shift, or roughly thirty dollars a month. Having experienced both sides as a picker and a small farmer with my father, I understood the hardships of agricultural laborers firsthand.

In 1915, I moved from the Livermore Valley to Richmond, California. This move marked a transition in my life after years of working as a picker and small farmer. Richmond was part of my journey before eventually becoming sheriff of Contra Costa County.

My mother was born in Hamburg, Germany, where her father served as the postmaster. She came to the United States around 1891 with her uncle, heading to Chicago in preparation for the 1892 World's Columbian Exposition. Hamburg was her original home and an important part of my family background.

My father was born in Denmark, in the province of Schleswig-Holstein. When Germany took over Denmark in 1870, he was nearly twenty and wanted to avoid military conscription, so he escaped by boarding a saltpeter boat near Hamburg. His journey eventually brought him to the United States, where he became an American citizen.

My mother came to Chicago around 1891 with her uncle to prepare for the World's Columbian Exposition in 1892. Chicago was the American city where she first settled after leaving Hamburg, Germany. It was a key stop in my family's immigration story.

The World's Columbian Exposition took place in 1892 in Chicago, and my mother came there in 1891 with her uncle to prepare for it. This event was significant in her immigration journey from Hamburg, Germany to the United States. It marked the beginning of our family's new life in America.

Germany played a big role in my family history. My mother was born in Hamburg, Germany, and my father was from the province of Schleswig-Holstein, which Germany took over in 1870. My father fled German military conscription by escaping on a ship near Hamburg before eventually making his way to the United States.

My father was born in Schleswig-Holstein, a province that Germany took over in 1870. To avoid military conscription by Germany, he escaped by boarding a saltpeter boat near Hamburg. This province was the starting point of his difficult journey to the United States.

During my father's escape from military conscription, he absconded from a saltpeter boat somewhere near Hamburg and ended up working his way to Chile. From there, he eventually made his way to North Carolina and then became an American citizen. Chile was an important stop in his long journey to freedom.

Richard Rains Veale was the sheriff of Contra Costa County for forty years before I succeeded him in 1934. He was an old-fashioned peace officer who ran the office in a horse-and-buggy style and was considered one of the best sheriffs we ever had. Even after losing the office, he remained a dedicated lawman until he passed away a few years later.
